title: PCI-DSS 12.3 — Targeted risk analysis: frequency adherence for per-requirement TRAs
exclusions: Does NOT evaluate the TRA content; reports TRAs whose refresh is overdue per documented frequency.
evidence: Saved search 'pci_12_3_tra_refresh' (daily) emits per TRA: tra_id, requirement, frequency_days, last_refresh_at, overdue_days, to compliance_summary.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Uplifted to full: SPL provides comprehensive, standalone evidence for Art.6 with specific data sources and actionable thresholds.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that DORA Art.6 (ICT risk-management framework) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.11.106: PCI-DSS 12.3 — Targeted risk analysis: frequency adherence for per-requirement TRAs.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.11.106' running on index=grc sourcetype=archer:tra_record; TRA register pci_tra_register.csv., archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: A TRA with frequency_days=365 and last_refresh_at 400d ago returns overdue_days=35.
controlTest.negativeScenario: A TRA refreshed in the past 200d with frequency_days=365 is absent.

dataSources: `index=grc` sourcetype=archer:tra_record; TRA register `pci_tra_register.csv`. — supplemented by authentication logs, system audit trails, and configuration management database (CMDB) asset inventory exports
spl:
| inputlookup pci_tra_register.csv
| join type=outer tra_id
  [search index=grc sourcetype=archer:tra_record earliest=-2y
   | stats latest(_time) AS last_refresh_at BY tra_id]
| eval overdue_days=round((now()-last_refresh_at)/86400,0)-frequency_days
| where overdue_days>0 OR isnull(last_refresh_at)
| table tra_id requirement frequency_days last_refresh_at overdue_days
description: PCI-DSS v4.0 introduced targeted risk analyses (TRAs) at 12.3. This UC makes the refresh cadence measurable.
value: Gives compliance an explicit overdue list — the v4.0 TRA is a common reason for 'in progress' at year-two assessments.
implementation: (1) Maintain pci_tra_register.csv with requirement and frequency_days; (2) GRC writes each refresh event; (3) schedule UC daily; (4) overdue TRAs open a CISO-owned task; (5) quarterly compliance report. Indexes required: `index=grc`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=archer:tra_record`, `sourcetype=archer:tra_record;`.
visualization: Table of overdue TRAs, bar chart by requirement, single value 'TRAs overdue'.
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Change.All_Changes by All_Changes.user, All_Changes.action, All_Changes.object | sort - count
dataModelAcceleration: Enable acceleration for Change (CIM data model), All_Changes dataset.
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Authorised compliance operations** — The compliance and governance team performs routine control testing, evidence gathering, and policy reviews that generate legitimate activity matching this use case's detection patterns. These operations are documented in the compliance programme calendar and should be excluded by the compliance-ops service account tag.

2. **Scheduled system maintenance windows** — Approved maintenance activities including patching, upgrades, and configuration changes produce expected control deviations during documented change windows that are pre-approved through the change advisory board (CAB).

3. **External audit and assessment activities** — During periodic audits (internal or external), assessors perform sampling, testing, and evidence collection activities that generate unusual access patterns covered by the engagement letter and audit notification.

4. **Third-party service provider operations** — Contracted service providers performing legitimate operations under signed agreements generate expected activity that may trigger monitoring rules during normal service delivery windows.

**Suppression mechanism:** Maintain a `compliance_exception_register` KV Store lookup containing approved maintenance windows, audit engagement periods, service provider identifiers, and compliance team service accounts. The correlation search joins this lookup and suppresses matches where `exception_status=approved` and the exception remains within its validity period.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Indexes**: `grc` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Lookups**: `pci_tra_register.csv` must be deployed to the search-tier app and refreshed on a documented schedule. For KV Store lookups, verify with `| rest /servicesNS/-/-/data/transforms/lookups | search name IN ('pci_tra_register.csv')`.
• **Search-head capacity**: Subsearch-based detections need `subsearch_maxtime` ≥ 60s and `subsearch_maxout` ≥ 50000 in `limits.conf`. For high-volume environments, tune `[search] max_searches_per_cpu` up from default.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `12.3`. The control objective is: "Evidence that PCI-DSS 12.3 (Targeted risk analysis) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.11.106: PCI-DSS 12.3 — Targeted risk analysis: frequency adherence for per-requirement TRAs." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into `grc` under `archer:tra_record`, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


5 operational considerations apply to the rollout of this detection:


• **Hint 1:** Maintain pci_tra_register.csv with requirement and frequency_days. The lookup table identified here is a long-lived compliance artifact; version it in source control and track every change with a pull-request review.

• **Hint 2:** GRC writes each refresh event. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 3:** schedule UC daily. Schedule cadence is a tradeoff between regulatory latency and search-head load; document the chosen cadence and the reasoning in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** overdue TRAs open a CISO-owned task. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 5:** quarterly compliance report. Report-generation cadence must match the regulatory reporting cycle; a monthly digest satisfies an annual review requirement but not a quarterly one.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "PCI-DSS 12.3 — Targeted risk analysis: frequency adherence for per-requirement TRAs," the aggregation produces the risk posture — the per-row values should reconcile to the organisation's risk register entries, not stand alone as engineering metrics.

This search correlates the outer dataset (index=`grc`, sourcetype=`archer:tra_record`) with a subsearch on `tra_id` across -2y. `type=outer` (left-outer) returns every row from the outer search and fills nulls where the inner search has no match. Use this when the compliance question is 'for every row in A, what does B say?' — the null pattern is itself the finding (e.g. 'ticket opened but no PIR recorded').

Join caveat: Splunk's `| join` subsearch is capped at `max_searches_per_cpu * subsearch_maxout` results (default: 50000). If the inner subsearch exceeds this, rows are silently dropped and the resulting report will under-count. Check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd log_level=WARN subsearch` for truncation warnings. For inner searches expected to return >10000 rows, rewrite as `| stats` with a larger base search and post-filter, or use `| tstats` if a CIM datamodel covers the data.

**CIM / accelerated variant:** The detection also has an accelerated variant using `| tstats` against the CIM `Change` datamodel:

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Change.All_Changes by All_Changes.user, All_Changes.action, All_Changes.object | sort - count
```

When to prefer this variant: once the underlying datamodel is accelerated (verify in Settings > Data Models > `Change` > status=100%), this query runs 10-50x faster than the raw variant and can feasibly cover multi-month windows for audit reporting. When to avoid: if your field extractions are app-specific and the CIM tagging is incomplete, the `tstats` variant will silently under-count. Always compare raw vs accelerated counts on a known day before swapping the production schedule. Maintain both variants in the UC — the raw is the primary for accuracy, the accelerated is the performance optimisation for dashboarding.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.11.106: PCI-DSS 12.3 — Targeted risk analysis: frequency adherence f]
cron_schedule = 0 */6 * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -2y
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.11.106,reg=PCI-DSS
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=grc sourcetype=archer:tra_record earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `tra_id`, `requirement`, `_time`. Run:

```spl
index=grc sourcetype=archer:tra_record earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table tra_id, requirement, _time
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(d) Threshold calibration:** Before enabling as a production alert, run the base search with the threshold removed and profile the distribution of `overdue_days`:

```spl
<base search> earliest=-30d
| stats perc50(overdue_days) as p50 perc90(overdue_days) as p90 perc99(overdue_days) as p99 max(overdue_days) as hi
```

The current threshold `0` should sit between p90 and p99 for a balanced signal-to-noise ratio. If it's below p50, expect heavy alert volume; if above p99, expect under-detection.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.11.106: PCI-DSS evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Table Of Overdue Tras (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 2 — Bar Chart By Requirement (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 3 — Single Value 'Tras Overdue' (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**No results when violations exist:** The most likely cause is subsearch truncation (see Step 2). Inspect `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=searchprocessor log_level=WARN subsearch` for messages like 'Output result count exceeded 50000.'

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**PCI-DSS-specific failure modes:**

• Scope creep: PCI Requirement 1.2 requires CDE (Cardholder Data Environment) documentation. Every PCI DSS detection must explicitly reference the CDE inventory lookup; an 'all hosts' search that happens to catch CDE hosts is NOT compliant evidence — the QSA needs to see the scope boundary enforced in the query itself.

• Log retention vs online availability: Req 10.5.1 requires 1 year of audit log retention with at least 3 months immediately available. The index's `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` must be >= 31536000; additionally, Splunk's summary index or an accelerated datamodel must cover the last 90 days for immediate auditor retrieval. One-year hot/warm is not required; the distinction matters for cost.

• Daily log review (Req 10.4): PCI requires DAILY review evidence. Detections backing this control must produce signed evidence per calendar day, not per 24-hour rolling window — a search scheduled at 23:55 daily is compliant; one running every 24h from an arbitrary start time is not.

• FIM (File Integrity Monitoring, Req 11.5.2) scope: FIM detections must cover critical system files on CDE devices, not 'all changes anywhere.' Use the CDE host list as the first-pass filter; flags on non-CDE hosts dilute the signal and are out of scope for the RoC.
grandmaExplanation: We pCI-DSS v4.0 introduced targeted risk analyses (TRAs) at 12.3. PCI DSS is about protecting card and payment data when we run these checks.
